documents.text: Java consumers connect to PostgreSQL through the pgjdbc JDBC driver, which is bundled inside each application rather than shared. PostgreSQL JDBC drivers older than 42.2.5 fail SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication, and builds before 42.5 may not negotiate the TLS defaults enforced by PostgreSQL 15+. Because the driver ships per-service, every Java integration (claims, billing, policy admin) must be independently rebuilt and redeployed with an updated pgjdbc. Connection-pool libraries (HikariCP) may also cache prepared statements that break on server-side plan changes.

A single PostgreSQL upgrade can affect consumers written in different languages very differently. A .NET reporting service using Npgsql or the psqlODBC driver requires Npgsql 6.0+ (for PG15) and an updated psqlODBC build to speak the new wire protocol and SCRAM authentication, while a Python ETL job on psycopg2 needs 2.9.3+, and a Java service needs pgjdbc 42.5+. These are independent upgrades owned by different teams with different release cadences, so the database change fans out into several coordinated client changes that must all land before cutover.

When multiple teams consume the same database, the schema and engine behavior become a shared contract that no single team fully owns. One team may read via a Java JDBC service, another via a Python data pipeline, and another via a BI tool over ODBC; each relies on specific type representations, extensions, or query-plan behavior. An engine upgrade that changes planner statistics, default authentication, or type output silently breaks a subset of consumers. Mitigation requires inventorying every dependent service, assigning named owners, running per-consumer integration tests, and gating the cutover on all consumers being validated together.

Cross-language data contracts break in subtle ways during upgrades. REST APIs backed by the upgraded system may change JSON serialization (timestamp precision, decimal/numeric formatting, null-vs-omitted fields, enum casing), silently breaking consumers in strictly-typed languages. Event-driven consumers reading Kafka topics depend on Avro/Protobuf schema-registry compatibility, and shared Parquet/Avro files written by Spark (Scala) and read by pandas (Python) can fail on schema evolution or type-width changes. Always run cross-language contract tests and diff representative payloads and files before and after the upgrade.
